title: Singlet-triplet oscillations in multivalley Si double quantum dots
authors: Łukasz Cywiński, Mats Volmer, Tom Struck, Giordano Scappucci, Lars R. Schreiber
abstract: Charge separation from the $(4,0)$ to the $(3,1)$ state in a Si/SiGe double quantum dot is commonly used for initialization of spin qubits and Pauli-spin-blockade readout. It was used in recent experiments involving creation of the $(3,1)$ singlet, and subsequent shuttling of one of the electrons. We present a theoretical description of the process of charge separation and singlet-triplet mixing, arriving at expressions for the singlet return probability that take into account experimentally observed finite probabilities of the creation of singlets with various patterns of valley occupations. In our analysis we focus on magnetic fields for which the electron spin Zeeman splitting is close to the valley splitting in one of the dots, when the spin-valley coupling causes a strong renormalization of the frequency of oscillations of singlet return probability. The latter effect has been recently used to perform valley splitting mapping by shuttling of one quantum dot to various locations with respect to the other. We give a detailed description of singlet-triplet dynamics near these spin-valley resonances and compare the results of calculations with measurements on double quantum dots in two distinct Si/SiGe heterostructures. Comparison of theory with experiments in which the presence of a few valley occupation patterns is visible, gives insight into the valley dependence of $g$-factors in these structures, providing support for a recently proposed theoretical model of this dependence. We also discuss how dephasing of singlet return probability oscillations near the spin-valley resonances is affected by valley splitting fluctuations caused by electric field noise.
summary: This paper investigates how spin-valley coupling and valley-dependent g-factors influence the dynamics of singlet-triplet oscillations in silicon double quantum dots. By modeling the charge separation process from (4,0) to (3,1) states, the authors show that spin-valley resonances significantly renormalize oscillation frequencies. They also identify that fluctuations in valley splitting, driven by electric field noise, are a primary source of dephasing. The theoretical model is validated by comparing it to experimental measurements in Si/SiGe heterostructures, providing insight into the valley-dependent nature of g-factors.
main_problem: Understanding the dynamics of singlet-triplet oscillations in Si/SiGe double quantum dots, specifically how spin-valley coupling and valley-splitting fluctuations affect qubit initialization and dephasing.
main_result: The study provides a theoretical framework for singlet return probability that accounts for multiple valley occupation patterns and demonstrates that valley-splitting fluctuations from electric field noise are a dominant dephasing mechanism near spin-valley resonances.
method: The authors use a multi-dimensional Hamiltonian approach (up to 17-dimensional) and Landau-Zener theory to model charge separation, comparing theoretical predictions of frequency renormalization and g-factor symmetry against experimental Fourier-transformed singlet return probability signals.
model_or_system: The system consists of two electrons in Si/SiGe or SiMOS double quantum dots (DQDs) transitioning between (4,0) and (3,1) charge states, modeled by a Hamiltonian including Zeeman splitting, valley splitting, and spin-valley coupling.
key_observables: Singlet return probability (Ps), singlet-triplet oscillation frequencies, and g-factor differences (delta g).
important_parameters: Valley splitting (Ev), Zeeman splitting (Ez), spin-valley coupling (v), tunnel coupling (tc), and charge noise-induced fluctuations (sigma_epsilon).
main_assumptions: The model assumes the lowest energy orbital states and the two lowest energy valley eigenstates per dot, and treats valley coupling components as uncorrelated Gaussian random variables.
figures_summary: Figures show Fourier transforms of singlet return probability for various datasets, illustrating multiple frequency components and the presence of different valley occupation patterns.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of spin-valley dynamics, presents a theoretical model for charge separation and singlet-triplet mixing, applies this model to analyze spin-valley resonances, compares results with experimental data from Si/SiGe heterostructures, and discusses dephasing mechanisms.
why_interesting: The paper explores the interplay between spin and valley degrees of freedom and the impact of environmental noise on quantum coherence, which is highly relevant to the study of open quantum systems and the development of robust spin qubits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Energies of singlet states (solid lines) and energies of spin-polarized (3, 1) triplet states (dashed lines) that can be mixed by spin-valley coupling with the singlets, calculated for 2µBB = ER V . Some of the spin-valley anticrossings (funnels) that occur at detunings at which the singlet is a superposition of (4, 0) and (3, 1) charge states are marked with circles. The 4 regions in which a (3, 1) singlet is mixed with a polarized triplet at large detuning are marked by ellipses. These are the spin-valley resonances at which the spin-valley coupling in the right dot affects the singlet dynamics in the far detuned regime, see the lines corresponding to odd r in Table I.
